coredata.dc:title: A self-enhanced ECL-RET immunosensor for the detection of CA19-9 antigen based on Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ - Amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as probe and graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide as platform
coredata.dc:creator: Bahari, Delnia
Babamiri, Bahareh
Salimi, Abdollah
Hallaj, Rahman
Amininasab, S. Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) assay as an efficient analytical technique has aroused considerable interest in recognition and biosensing. In the present study, a novel self-enhanced ECL-RET of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ as an efficient luminophore to the MoS2 nanosheets as effective quencher was designed for CA19-9 antigen analysis. Herein, the graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (GO-HBP) with high loading ability for Ru-complex was used as the sensing platform, while amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) which covalently linked to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ applied as co-reactant for enhancing of anodic ECL signal response. In this approach GO-HBP-Ru-complex-NCND-anti-CA19-9 Ab exhibited amplified ECL emissions (“on” state) and with formation of sandwiched immunocomplex between, immobilized CA19-9 Ab, CA19-9 antigen and MoS2 nanosheets modified with CA19 9 antibody, the ECL response of luminophore was efficiently quenched (“off” state). The signal response was doubly amplified by covalent attachment of more luminophore, co-reactant and CA19-9 antibody with hyperbranched aromatic polyamide. On the basis of all above features, the ECL intensity of Ru-NCND decreased with the increase of the concentration of CA19-9 antigen in a wide linear range of 2 mU mL−1- 50 U mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.25 mU mL−1 (S/N = 3). The application of the fabricated ECL-RET immunosensor for determination of CA19-9 antigen in human serum samples was appraised that the satisfactory results were found to be in admissible accord with those gained with the reference method (ELISA assay).The suggested method opens up a novel avenue for expanding high-performance ECL-RET immunosensors cancer markers detection in clinical monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET)
Immunosensor CA19-9 antigen
Ru-complex/N doped carbon dots luminophore
GO- Highly branched polyamide platform

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nano dots (NCNDs) and MoS2 nanoflowers
Preparation of the graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide
Morphological and structural characterization of MoS2 nanoflowers (NFs) and graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (GO-HBP)
The amplification of proposed immunosensor investigated by ECL characterization
Possible mechanism for the ECL response of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)- NCNDs
Performance of the ECL immunosensor in CA19-9 detection
Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the immunosensor
Detection of CA19-9 antigen in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NCND, (B) UV–Vis absorption of NCNDs and PL spectra of NCNDs with different excitation wavelengths, (C) XPS scanning spectrum shows three major peaks of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. XPS high resolution survey scan of (D) C1s, (E) N1s and (F) O1s region of CDs.
(A, B) SEM images of MoS2 nanoflowers and GO-HBP(C, D).

(A) UV–Vis absorption of (a) NCNDs, (b) Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) and (c) Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs, (B) ECL emission of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)/NCNDs (a), Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)/TPrA (b), Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-covalently attached to NCNDs (c), (C) ECL response of Nafion/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs(a) and Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs and (D) Nyquist plots at at various electrodes (a) bare ITO (Inset carve), (b)(a) + Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru-NCND, (c)(b) + Ab1CA19-9, (d) (c) + BSA, (e) (d) + AgCA19-9 and (f) (e)+ MoS2-Ab2, recorded in a solution of 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]2−/[Fe(CN)6]3−, applied potential was 0.25 V and the frequency range was from 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz.

(A) ECL responses of the fabricated immunosensor in various concentrations range of CA19-9 antigen from 2 mU mL−1 to 50 U mL−1 in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS, scanning from 0.0 V to 1.5 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, (B) The corresponding relationship between the ECL intensity and the logarithm of CA19-9 concentration, (C) The stability of the ECL-RET developed sensor based Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru-NCND/Ab1CA19-9/AgCA19-9/MoS2-Ab2 system with incubation 40 mU/mL CA19-9 antigen under 10 consecutive cyclic potential scans, (D) The ECL intensities response of the immunosensor toward various tumor markers including CA15-3, CA125, HBsAg and CEA.
(A–D) Different steps for synthesis of probe and platform. (E) Schematic representation of the ECL biosensor for CA19-9 detection.
Synthesis of graphene oxide grafted hyper branched aromatic polyamide.
Comparison the analytical performance of different methods for CA19-9 Ag detection.
A recovery study carried out in serum samples for CA19-9 Ag detection by the proposed immunosensor.

head.ce:title: A self-enhanced ECL-RET immunosensor for the detection of CA19-9 antigen based on Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ - Amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as probe and graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide as platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) assay as an efficient analytical technique has aroused considerable interest in recognition and biosensing. In the present study, a novel self-enhanced ECL-RET of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ as an efficient luminophore to the MoS2 nanosheets as effective quencher was designed for CA19-9 antigen analysis. Herein, the graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (GO-HBP) with high loading ability for Ru-complex was used as the sensing platform, while amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) which covalently linked to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ applied as co-reactant for enhancing of anodic ECL signal response. In this approach GO-HBP-Ru-complex-NCND-anti-CA19-9 Ab exhibited amplified ECL emissions (“on” state) and with formation of sandwiched immunocomplex between, immobilized CA19-9 Ab, CA19-9 antigen and MoS2 nanosheets modified with CA19 9 antibody, the ECL response of luminophore was efficiently quenched (“off” state). The signal response was doubly amplified by covalent attachment of more luminophore, co-reactant and CA19-9 antibody with hyperbranched aromatic polyamide. On the basis of all above features, the ECL intensity of Ru-NCND decreased with the increase of the concentration of CA19-9 antigen in a wide linear range of 2 mU mL−1- 50 U mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.25 mU mL−1 (S/N = 3). The application of the fabricated ECL-RET immunosensor for determination of CA19-9 antigen in human serum samples was appraised that the satisfactory results were found to be in admissible accord with those gained with the reference method (ELISA assay).The suggested method opens up a novel avenue for expanding high-performance ECL-RET immunosensors cancer markers detection in clinical monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Statistical studies done by the American Cancer Society in 2016 have shown that due to the lack of early and accurate diagnostic tools, the five-year survival rate of pancreatic cancer (PC) patients is less than 8%, which is almost the lowest among existing cancers [1]. Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) is a glycoprotein biomarker for the timely diagnosis and clinical monitoring of pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer, gastric carcinoma, and particularly pancreatic carcinoma [2,3]. Nowadays, the detection methods of tumor markers include radioimmunoassay [4], electrochemical immunoassay [5,6], chemiluminescence assay [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8], fluorescence method and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay have been established to quantify trace amounts of CA19-9. Among them, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) assay, due to its advantages, which include wide dynamic concentration response range, high detection sensitivity, favorable simplicity and low background signal has shown to be a reassuring technique in immunoassay field, drug evaluation and environmental monitoring [9–11]. Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) is the production of light from a high-energy electron-transfer reaction between an appropriately matched donor and acceptor pair at the nanometer-scale (typically less than 10 nm), and it has been used for DNA detection [12,13], cytosensing [14,15], and immunoassay [16,17]. As one of the most universal ECL systems in analytical applications, Ru(bpy)3 2+-TPrA possessed its distinct advantages of the great electrochemiluminescence performance in aqueous system, reversible electrochemical manner and chemical stability [9,18,19]. However, TPrA not only is toxic, corrosive, and volatile, but also high concentrations of it are required (usually up to 100 mM) to obtain good sensitivity in ECL based measuring systems. Therefore, an effective efforts has been done to introduce alternative co-reactants with matching redox potentials, good solubility, high chemical stability, rapid degradation routes and swift charge transfer kinetics [20].

Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel kind of quasi-spherical fluorescent nanomaterials, have been used in development of novel fluorometric sensors/biosensors [21,22]. Nitrogen-doped carbon nano dots (NCND), which have high solubility in water, excellent chemical stability, matching redox potentials with Ru(bpy)3 2+, fast charge transfers kinetics and quick degradation path to create high energy radical, can be used as a good alternative to TPrA [23]. The NCND as convenient co-reactant can be covalently bonded to a metal-organic complex, especially classic Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivative for increasing of anodic ECL emission due to electron transfer shorter route and decreasing of energy loss. Furthermore, the self-enhanced ECL system with simplified design exhibits the performance and stability of ECL assays [24].

The hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (HBP) and polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer with unique properties such as multiple branch ends accessible for further conjugation, outstanding thermal and mechanical properties can be used for immobilizing capture probes (CP) and ECL labels [25–28], while HBP or dendrimers as carriers of ECL labels can decline the conductivity of the modified probe. To overcome this limitation, different nanomaterials such as graphene oxides (GO), quantum dots (QD) and gold nanoparticles have been used to enhance the electrical conductivity of the electrochemical platform in biosensors design [29–31]. Furthermore, immobilizing of Ru(bpy)3 2+ onto nanomaterials, can improved ECL efficiency and also sensor stability [16].

In the past few years, two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 nanosheets owing to their outstanding electronic, optical and mechanical properties have attracted increasing consideration in numerous research fields such as sensing, catalytic processes and energy storage [22,32]. Notably, the MoS2 nanosheets has been applied to expand ECL based biosensors for determination of heavy metals, hydrogen peroxide and different biomolecules duo to their high quenching capability and good biocompatibility [33–35].

In this study, continuing a previous study on the development of ECL immunosensors/biosensors [36–39] we examined the rapid and highly sensitive self-enhanced ECL-RET system for the detection of CA19-9 tumor maker using graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (GO-HBP) as the sensing platform, Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ as luminescent label and NCND as co-reactant agent. This strategy is illustrated in Scheme 1 . At first, Nafion-GO-HBP labeled Ru-NCNDs located on the surface of ITO electrode. After incubation of CA19-9 antigens and MoS2-Ab2 bioconjugates, resonance energy transfers from Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ to the MoS2 nanosheets leads to ECL quenching of Ru complex. The obtained results demonstrated that NCNDs in covalently linked systems with Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+, due to an intramolecular electron transfer reaction, act as effective co-reactant in the ECL process. The proposed GO-HBP-Ru-complex-NCND-anti-CA19-9 nanoarchitecture illustrated a considerably amplified ECL signal (“On” state), while after sandwiched immunocomplex formation, the ECL signal response effectively quenched (“Off” state) due to ERT process between Ru-complex and MoS2 nanosheets as donor and acceptor, respectively. The decreasing of ECL signal in the presence of different concentration of CA19-9 cancer marker is measured and the analytical ability of the introduced assay for measurement of cancer marker in human serum samples is also evaluated.

The CA19-9 EIA kit was ordered from CanAg Diagnostics AB, Gothenburg, Sweden. It consisted of CA19-9 standards and a solution containing monoclonal anti-CA19-9 antibodies. Ruthenium(III) chloride hydrate (RuCl3.3H2O), 1,10-Phenanthrolin-5-amine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), ammonium hexafluorophosphate (NH4PF6), tri-n-propylamine (TPrA), (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane, triphenyl phosphate (TPP) and 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid (DABA), ammonium molybdate ((NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O), hydrazine monohydrate (N2H4.H2O) and sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S.9H2O) were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich. Creatine, guanidinium chloride, dimethylbenzene, pyridine, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), glutaraldehyde (GA 25%), nafion, NaOH and HCl with analytical reagent grade were also ordered from Merck. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off = 1000 Da) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The serum sample containing CA19-9 marker from a clinically diagnosed patient with pancreatic cancer and the detected ELISA results were provided by Verdi Hematological Clinic Center, Sanandaj, Iran. In the ECL process, the phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.40), composed of 0.10 M NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 + 0.10 M NaCl was applied as the detecting buffer throughout the study. Double-distilled water was applied all over this experimental process.

All applied electrochemical and electrochemiluminescence systems for ECL measurements, and also the techniques and instruments for characterization of nanomaterials and composites used reported in SI file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nano dots (NCNDs) and MoS2 nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: The details of procedure were applied for synthesis of amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and MoS2 nanoflowers reported in SI file.

The conjugation of Ab2 with MoS2 was done based on the procedure common in the literature, with some modification. Typically, 1 mL of the MoS2 (0.5 mg mL-1) that optimized sonicated for 2 h to attain a homogeneous solution. Then, 1 mL of 0.1 mg mL-1 Ab2 was added to the mentioned mixture and stirred comprehensively for 1 h followed by overnight incubation at 4 °C to produce the MoS2-Ab2 bioconjugates. Self-assembly connection is the main reason for the conjugation of MoS2 with Ab2. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and washed with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). After washing, the MoS2-Ab2 was dispersed in the PBS solution and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

Firstly, the Ru(bpy)2Cl2 was synthesized based on the method discussed in the prior publication, then synthesis of [Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)] was performed by a modified literature procedure [40] as follows: In a typical experiment, Ru(bpy)2Cl2 (0.064 mmol) in 5 mL hot water and phen-NH2 (0.072 mmol) in 10 mL hot ethanol were dissolved and refluxed at 100 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained mixture was filtered and concentrated, then 10 mL of deionized water and saturated solution of NH4PF6 were added to the residue. The orange precipitates of [Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)] were obtained (scheme 1B).

The Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) was covalently bound to the NCNDs as follows: 5 mg of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) was added into 1 mL of water, and then 1 mL of glutaraldehyde solution (2.5 wt %) was added to the solution and steadily shaken for 10 min at room temperature. Then, 1 mL of NCNDs (5 mg mL-1) was added into the aldehyde-functionalized Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) and gently stirred for 6h at room temperature. Finally, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove unbound NCNDs and then re-dispersed in 1 mL PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the graphene oxide (GO) was obtained based on the modified Hummers approach, then graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide was prepared according to the previous reported procedure with some modification [26]. In the following text, graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide was suggested as graphene oxide-HBP. Typically, 0.76 g graphene oxide and 0.76 g 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid (DABA) were charged in 10 mL of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone under vigorous stirring for 30 min. Then, 2.5 mL of pyridine and 2.6 mL of triphenyl phosphate (TPP) were added to the above mixture solution. In the following, the obtained mixture was transferred into a 500 mL round flask and the temperature was gradually enhanced to 100 °C and stirred under nitrogen atmosphere for 4 h. In the following, after cooling naturally, the solution was shredded into 60 mL of methanol to precipitate the graphene oxide-HBP. Finally, the obtained graphene oxide-HBP was filtered and purified by reprecipitation from dimethylformamide solution into methanol including 0.1% lithium chloride (LiCl). The prepared graphene oxide-HBP was finally washed by cold methanol and then dried in a vacuum at 100 °C to again a constant weight. The procedure of GO-HBP synthesis is shown as Scheme 2 .

The construction process of the ECL immunoassay is shown in scheme 1C. Firstly, the mixture of Nafion-graphene oxide-HBP by mixing 50 μL of Nafion (10%, diluted by ethanol) and 500 μL of graphene oxide-HBP (0.5 mg mL-1) and sonicating for 30 min was prepared. Then, 100 μL homogeneous composite of Nafion-graphene oxide-HBP was located on the surface of cleaned ITO electrode and dried for obtaining Nafion-graphene oxide-HBP modified ITO. The terminal amine (-NH2) groups of the graphene oxide-HBP-modified electrode surface were activated in a solution containing GA (2.5% wt) for 30 min and incubated with 100 μL of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs (0.1 mg mL-1) for 1 h at 4 °C. After washing thoroughly with PBS to eliminate physically absorbed Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs, the modified electrode was immersed in a 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 mg mL-1 Ab1CA19-9 at 37ᵒC for 30 min. After three times dipping the modified electrode into a PBS solution (pH 7.4) to removing the unbound antibodies, the modified ITO electrode was incubated in a 1.0 % wt of BSA solution for 30 min to block the nonspecific binding sites. The modified electrode was then immersed in the PBS solution containing several concentrations of CA 19-9 for 20 min at 37 °C and washed with PBS to eliminate the physically adsorbed antigen. Finally, with incubating of MoS2-Ab2 for 30 min sandwich type immunosensor was fabricated and then washed thoroughly with PBS solution to remove nonspecifically bounded.

In a typical ECL assay, the modified electrode with various concentrations of CA19-9 was immersed in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) and the ECL measurements was done via an anodic ECL pathway with a three-electrode system. The photomultiplier tube voltage was set at 800 V by placing a band-pass filter of 617 nm (bandwidth, 10 nm) before the PMT window, and potential scanning was done from 0.0 V to 1.5 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4).

The morphological characteristics of the as-prepared NCNDs was investigated by taking the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 A). TEM images revealed that the diameters of the monodispersed and spherical NCNDs were in the range of 5–8 nm. The optical property of the prepared NCNDs were investigated by recording of fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra. Fig. 1B, indicated the typical absorption and emission spectra of the obtained NCND at different excitation wavelengths from 200 nm to 450 nm. The NCNDs exhibited a substantial UV–Vis absorption band centered at nearly 320 nm, which most probably corresponded to the n - π∗ transition of CO bonds and the maximum fluorescence emission intensity (440 nm) obtained at the excitation wavelength at 320 nm. The FT-IR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the chemical bonds and functional groups of the NCNDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the infrared peaks at 1164 and 1342 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–O–C and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption bands of C–N and CO located at 1506 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1, respectively that this bands illustrated the existence of amide groups. Also, the infrared peaks at 3020 and 2843 cm−1 due to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of C–H and the broad peak located at ∼3422 cm−1 attributed to stretching of the amino group (NH2). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were also applied to better confirm the functional groups and the surface of analysis of NCNDs. The full-scan XPS spectra of the as-prepared NCND confirmed that three characteristic peaks were observed at 282.36, 398.18 and 528.38 eV, which were attributed to the C1s, N1s and O1s binding peak, respectively (Fig. 1C). The XPS spectrum of C1s can be deconvoluted into four surface components corresponding to sp3 (C–C/C–H) at 284.95, sp3(C–OH/C–O–C) at 286.35, sp2 (CO) at 287.85 and sp2 (O–CO) at 288.85 (Fig. 1D). The N1s peaks at 399.15, 400.55 and 401eV demonstrated in Fig. 1E are associated with nitrogen in the states NH2, N-(CO)–O and N–C3, respectively. This results indicated the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 1F, the chemical state of oxygen according to the XPS O1s spectrum are assigned to N–CO at 531.15 eV, C–O–C at 532.95 eV and O–CO∗ at 533.94 eV. The amino groups of the NCNDs were covalently coupled with the amino groups of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ using GA as the linker agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of MoS2 nanoflowers (NFs) and graphene oxide grafted hyperbranched aromatic polyamide (GO-HBP)
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological structure of the synthesized MoS2 was characterized by taking of SEM (Fig. 2 A and B). As illustrated, the MoS2 exhibits a nanoflower-like structure with a diameter of several hundred nanometers, assembled by very thin thickness nanosheets. For further verification purity of synthesized MoS2, the EDX spectrum was recorded (Fig. S2) and corresponding peaks of Mo and S in the prepared sample, clearly confirmed the purity of MoS2 nanostructures. The infrared spectrum of the GO-HBP is given in Fig. S3. The absorption bands of asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of CH2 observed at 2925 and 2854 cm−1, respectively. Also, the wavenumbers of 1436, 1548 and 1625 cm−1 are due to the CH2, N–H and CO bending vibration, respectively. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 3419 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching of the amino group (NH2). The surface morphology of the GO-HBP is studied by taking SEM images (Fig. 2C and D) and the fractured paths on the graphene oxide indicated the formation of hyperbranched polymer on the GO.

Figure S0034 shows the recorded FT-IR spectra of Ru(bpy)2Cl2 (a), phen-NH2 (b) and Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) (c), respectively. As can be observed in Fig. S4, the absorption band at 3454 and 3388 cm−1 corresponds to NH2 group of phen-NH2 which presented in Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) structure and confirm the successful formation of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) molecule. Also, the successful conjugation of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) and NCNDs were studied by recording of the optical absorption spectra of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2), NCNDs and Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, pure NCNDs exhibits evident absorption peak at 320 nm corresponding to n - π∗ transition of CO bond. Furthermore, Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) illustrates two maxima at 285 nm and 460 nm. The appearance of three strong absorption bands at 285, 320 and 460 nm (curve c of Fig. 3A) confirmed the successful conjugation of NCNDs to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The amplification of proposed immunosensor investigated by ECL characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemiluminescence signal increases greatly using amines as co-reactant in the order primary < secondary < tertiary. Fig. 3B demonstrates the ECL intensity of the NCNDs at 0.1 mg/mL in comparison to the ordinary co-reactant TPrA at the same concentration. The ECL signal of the immunosensor with TPrA (curve b) was slightly superior to NCNDs (curve a). Furthermore, the ECL emission intensity of NCNDs as co-reactant agent when covalently linked to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) is two times higher with respect to uncoupled components (curve c), owing to shorter electron-transfer path and minor energy loss in Ru-NCNDs couple, molecular electron transfer is more efficacious compared to the intermolecular reaction. Graphene oxide-HBP with many tertiary amine groups have plenty of binding sites to bind a large amount of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs, so graphene oxide-HBP acted as a carrier for the immobilization of more Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs and also CA19-9 antibodiy. As shown in Fig. 3C, when the luminescent labels were conjugated with the graphene oxide-HBP (curve b), the ECL signal is 3.0 folds higher than that of Nafion/Ru-NCND (curve a) when it was used as sensing platform. The obtained results illustrated that the application of NCND, graphene oxide-HBP and Ru-complex as integrated chemical system can be used as efficient sensing platform, with the high performance for immobilizing capture probes (CP) and ECL labels.

It has been demonstrated that electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a successful approach to follow the modification processes that has been broadly used to study the immunosensor fabrication. Therefore, to investigate the changes of electrode behavior at each modification step, Nyquist plots were carried out in 0.01 M PBS solution containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the bare ITO electrode revealed a very small semicircle domain (curve a). With coating the surface of ITO electrode by Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru-NCND, the resistance increased (curve b). In the following, persistent increases of the resistance were attained by the stepwise assembly of Ab1CA19-9 and BSA (curves c and d, respectively) which denoted that the Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru-NCND/Ab1CA19-9 was successively blocked by BSA. Similarly, the electron-transfer resistance continuously increased when the Nafion/GO-HBP/Ru-NCND/Ab1CA19-9 successively incubated with CA19-9 antigen. Finally, with incubating the modified electrode by the MoS2-Ab2 bioconjugate, the resistance increased significantly (curve f), which confirmed the successful formation of sandwiched immunocomplex during fabrication of ECL immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the ECL response of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)- NCNDs
ce:section.ce:para:
For the proposed assay, electrochemiluminescence properties of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ at ITO electrode were studied and the suggested possible mechanism sketched in Scheme 1. The covalent attachment of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ fluorophore and the amino groups of NCNDs as co-reactant enhanced the ECL of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+. Both Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ and NCND in the Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)- NCNDs complex were oxidized during the potential scanning from 0.0 to 1.5V. Then at 1.2 V in the ECL potential curve, the NCND•- Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+ converted to excited-state NCND- Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+∗ that could generate higher intensity light emission by intramolecular electron transfer and energy transition [41]. The corresponding ECL mechanisms are described as follows:
(1) NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+- e−→ NCND -Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+
(2) NCND -Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+-e-→ NCNDH•+-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+
(3) NCNDH•+-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+ - H+→ NCND• -Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+
NCND•-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2++NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+→
(4) NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+∗ + NCND•+-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+
(5) NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+∗ → NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ + hν

In order to get better sensing performances of the fabricated immunosensor, the assay conditions, including, incubation time of antigen with related antibody, the incubation time of MoS2-Ab2 CA 19-9 with antigen, the concentration of NCND, Ru-NCND, MoS2 and GO-HBP on the ECL intensity were optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S5. A, the ECL intensity gently decreased with the increasing CA 19-9 reaction time, and the ECL intensity tended to constant after 20 min. Thus, the optimal reaction time was 20 min throughout subsequent experiment. Also, the effects of incubation time of MoS2-Ab2 CA 19-9 with CA19-9 antigen on the ECL intensity were studied by recording of ECL signal at the range 10–60 min (Fig. S5. B.). The obtained results indicated that the ECL intensity decreased with increasing incubation time and after 30 min started to a plateau. Therefore, 30 min was applied as the optimal incubation time for further investigation. Also, the effect of GO-HBP concentration on ECL signal of the immunosensor is shown in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. S5.C, the ECL response increased with increasing of GO-HBP concentration at range 0.1–0.5 mg/mL, and a plateau was reached after 0.5 mg/mL, demonstrating that 0.5 mg/mL was sufficient for biosensing process. Similarly, the ECL intensities also increased with increasing the amount of NCND from 1 to 5 mg/mL and had a slight difference above 5 mg/mL (Fig. S5. D). Furthermore, with the increase of Ru-NCND concentration, the signal response increased and the maximum of the ECL signal was observed at 0.1 mg/mL of Ru-NCND, and decreasing of ECL signal started at concentrations of higher than this value, so, 0.1 mg/mL of Ru-NCND was chosen for following experiment. The effect of MoS2 concentration on the ECL-RET signal response was also optimized and as illustrated in Fig. 5S (graph E), the optimum concentration value of MoS2 for proposed immunosensor was 0.5 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the ECL immunosensor in CA19-9 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative behavior of the immunosensor was investigated by recording the dependency of ECL intensity on the concentration of the CA19-9 antigens under optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the electrochemiluminescence emission decreased regularly with increasing concentration of CA19-9 antigens after introducing MoS2-Ab2 to the electrode surface and leading to ECL intensity quenching of Ru-NCND composite, due to the high quenching yield of MoS2. So, as shown in Fig. S6, the MoS2 nanoflowers can efficiently quench the ECL intensity of Ru-NCND composite that indicated the high quenching efficiency of MoS2 nanoflowers [42]. Accordingly, the response of ECL was proportionate to the logarithm of varied CA19-9 antigen concentrations from 2 mU mL−1 to 50 U mL−1 range with the detection limit of 0.25 mU mL−1 (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4B). The analytical properties of the presented ECL assay for CA19-9 detection with other determination methods are displayed in Table 1 A. As can be seen the performance of this immunosensor is comparable to or better than other CA19-9 assays. These results illustrated that the proposed ECL-RET strategy is particularly sensitive and has excellent potential for precise determination of CA19-9 antigens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and specificity of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability, reproducibility and specificity are the important factors to evaluate the performance of the immunosensors. The stability of the immunosensor was examined under consequence cyclic potential scanning for 10 cycles (Fig. 4 C). The results show the good ECL stability of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)-NCNDs system with a relative standard deviation of 3.5% under 10 consequence cyclic potential scans, suggesting that the present immunosensor had a very stable ECL response towards CA19-9 antigen determination. Furthermore, when the proposed immunosensor was stored in 4 °C for two weeks, the sensor response showed 90% of its initial response. Also, reproducibility of the immunosensor for CA19-9 antigen was evaluated by measuring the same CA19-9 antigen sample for 5 replicate measurements. The relative standard deviations (RSD) was no more than 5%, indicating the good reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor. To manifest the specificity of the ECL immunosensor, the response of the immunosensor toward several substances including CA15-3, CA125, HBsAg and CEA with concentration of one hundred times higher than the concentration of CA19-9 (50 U/mL) were tested. As indicated in Fig. 4D, only in the presence of CA19-9 antigens, the remarkable ECL change response was observed and the other investigated substances had negligible effect on ECL intensity revealing the superior specificity of the ECL-RET immunosensor for CA19-9 Ag detection. Furthermore to show the cross-reactivity between two tumor markers the ECL response of the proposed immunosensor incubating with a mixture of CA19-9 and other antigens including; CA15-3, CA125, HBsAg and CEA was recorded. The ECL intensities were similar for mixtures of CA 19-9 with other antigens and CA 19-9 alone (Fig. S7). These results illustrated that the cross-reactivity between the two tumor markers was negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CA19-9 antigen in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: For distinguishing the possibility and potential applicability of the purposed ECL-RET system, the fabricated immunosensor was utilized to the measurement of CA19-9 tumor marker in serum samples with the standard addition method and the acquired results were evaluated with clinical laboratory analysis (ELISA) as the reference method. The human serum samples were gained from the Verdi Clinical laboratory in Sanandaj, Iran. As can be seen in Table B, the recoveries were in the range of 96.20%–98.27% with the RSD in the range of 3.21%–4.03%. The interaction of coexisting compounds in the serum sample with graphene oxide based electrochemical platform or MoS2 nanosheets as quencher may decrease the ECL signal response or deactivation of FRET process and detection of CA19-9 in the real samples with some loss (recovery <100%). Also, there is no considerable dissimilarity between the results attained from the suggested immunosensor and the ELISA method for detection of CA19-9 antigen. The obtained results illustrated that the fabricated ECL-RET immunosensor can be employed for efficient measurement of the CA19-9 antigen in human serum samples for clinical application.

In summary, the sandwich-type ECL-RET assay was designed for the detection of the CA19-9 antigen using Nafion/GO-HBP as the amplifying system in sensor platform, and Ru-complex as effective ECL emitter. Graphene oxide significantly promoted the electron transfer and HBP was used as the carrier for the immobilization of more Ru- complex as luminophore and enhancing the ECL signal. Furthermore, NCNDs as effective co-reactant covalently linked with Ru-complex was used for reducing the reaction time and improving the efficiency of electron transfer. The obtained results demonstrated that the quenching ECL immunosensor in view of ECL-RET between MoS2 and Ru-NCND system was integrated for measurement of the proposed antigen. In addition, high sensitivity and good anti-interference capability, excellent stability, acceptable precision and accuracy, greatly improved the efficiency of the immunosensor for bioanalysis purposes. Finally, we demonstrated the ability of the presented integrated chemical system (containing GO-HBP, NCND, Ru-complex and MoS2 nanosheets) can improve analytical characteristics of immunosensors and determination of cancer markers in real samples with satisfactory results. As a result, the proposed strategy opens new windows in the development of noteworthy analytical tools for the measurement of tumor markers in clinical laboratories.

Delnia Bahari: doing the experiment, writing, Formal analysis. Bahareh Babamiri: synthesis and characterization of NCND, real sample, Formal analysis. Abdollah Salimi: Supervision, Reviewing and Editing. Rahman Hallaj: set up of ECL system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Research Office of University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086) and Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Funds for research on nanomaterials based biosensors. We are also grateful to Dr. Farzaneh Verdi (Verdi Hematological Clinic Center manager) for accommodating serum samples for analysis and also for the measuring of CA19-9 antigen in real sample by ELISA method.
